sources: ISSN Position Stand: Creatine Supplementation (JISSN, 2017) — doi:10.1186/s12970-017-0173-z
ISSN Position Stand: Caffeine and Exercise Performance (JISSN, 2021) — doi:10.1186/s12970-021-00383-4
ISSN Position Stand: Protein and Exercise (JISSN, 2017) — doi:10.1186/s12970-017-0177-8

evidence_grades.A: Strong evidence — multiple well-designed RCTs consistently support use
evidence_grades.B: Moderate evidence — some RCTs support use, more research needed
evidence_grades.C: Limited evidence — preliminary or conflicting results
evidence_grades.D: No credible evidence or evidence of harm

rule: Only recommend supplements graded A or B. Never recommend brands. Always ask goal and health context first.

creatine_monohydrate.what_it_does: Increases phosphocreatine stores, enabling higher-intensity effort and faster recovery between sets.
creatine_monohydrate.who_benefits: Strength and power athletes, HIIT, anyone doing repeated high-intensity efforts.
creatine_monohydrate.dose: 3–5 g/day consistently. Loading phase (20g/day for 5 days) accelerates saturation but is optional.
creatine_monohydrate.timing: Timing is flexible — post-workout with a meal is a common choice but evidence is not strong on timing.
creatine_monohydrate.vegetarian_note: Vegetarians have lower baseline muscle creatine — likely to see larger performance gains than omnivores.
creatine_monohydrate.safety: Well-tolerated in healthy adults. Increase fluid intake. Not recommended for those with kidney disease.

caffeine.what_it_does: Reduces perceived effort, improves endurance, strength, and focus. One of the most studied ergogenic aids.
caffeine.who_benefits: Endurance athletes, HIIT, strength training, any sport or gym session over 30 min.
caffeine.dose: 3–6 mg per kg body weight (e.g. 180–360 mg for a 60 kg person). Start at lower end.
caffeine.timing: 45–60 minutes before exercise for peak plasma levels.
caffeine.natural_sources: Strong brewed coffee (100–150 mg per cup), espresso (~60 mg per shot), green tea (30–50 mg).
caffeine.cautions: Avoid within 6 hours of sleep. Can cause anxiety, palpitations, GI discomfort at high doses. Not for those with anxiety disorders or heart arrhythmias.

protein_powder.what_it_does: Convenient high-quality protein to hit daily targets, especially post-workout.

types.whey: Fast-digesting, highest leucine content, ideal post-workout for non-vegetarians.
types.casein: Slow-digesting, ideal before bed to sustain MPS overnight.
types.plant_based: Soy protein is complete and comparable to whey. Pea + rice blend gives a complete amino acid profile.
types.important: Whole food first — powder fills gaps, not the foundation.

protein_powder.vegetarian_vegan: Soy protein isolate or pea+rice blend are the best plant options. Avoid single-source rice protein (low leucine).
protein_powder.dose: 20–40 g per serving to maximise MPS. Total daily intake counts more than timing.

beta_alanine.what_it_does: Increases muscle carnosine, buffering acid buildup during high-intensity exercise lasting 1–4 minutes.
beta_alanine.who_benefits: HIIT, rowing, cycling, swimming, combat sports — high-intensity efforts in the 1–4 minute range.
beta_alanine.dose: 3.2–6.4 g/day divided into smaller doses (0.8–1.6 g) to reduce tingling (paraesthesia).
beta_alanine.cautions: Common side effect is harmless tingling/flushing. Use slow-release form to minimise.

magnesium.what_it_does: Supports muscle function, reduces cramping, aids sleep quality and overnight recovery.
magnesium.who_benefits: Active individuals, especially vegetarians (plant foods have magnesium but also phytates that reduce absorption).
magnesium.dose: 200–400 mg/day from food + supplement combined. Magnesium glycinate or citrate are well-absorbed forms.
magnesium.food_first: Pumpkin seeds (156 mg/oz), almonds, spinach, quinoa, dark chocolate.
magnesium.cautions: High supplemental doses (>350 mg/day) may cause loose stools. Start with food sources.

vitamin_d.what_it_does: Supports muscle function, immune health, calcium absorption, and bone integrity.
vitamin_d.who_benefits: Anyone in low-sunlight regions, indoor athletes, vegetarians/vegans (limited dietary sources).
vitamin_d.dose: 1000–2000 IU/day D3 (cholecalciferol) in autumn/winter. Test serum 25(OH)D first if possible.
vitamin_d.vegan_form: D2 (ergocalciferol) or lichen-derived D3 for vegans.
vitamin_d.cautions: Fat-soluble — take with a meal containing fat. Excess intake (>4000 IU/day) can be toxic.
vitamin_d.source: NIH ODS — Vitamin D Fact Sheet

what_not_to_recommend: Fat burners / thermogenics — no credible evidence, often stimulant-heavy and poorly regulated
Testosterone boosters — no evidence for most ingredients; potential harm
HGH supplements — no credible OTC product works; potential harm
Any supplement for a medical condition (diabetes, thyroid, kidney disease) — refer to doctor
ask_before_recommending: What is the user's primary goal? (strength, endurance, fat loss, general health)
Are they vegetarian/vegan? (changes protein and creatine recommendations)
Do they have any health conditions or medications? (some supplements interact)
Are they already taking any supplements? (avoid overlap or antagonism)
